description: 15 manually curated QA pairs for evaluating multi-agent RAG system
source: arXiv research papers on transformers and attention mechanisms

questions.question: What is the attention mechanism in transformer models?
questions.answer: Attention mechanism allows models to focus on relevant parts of input sequences by computing weighted relationships between all positions simultaneously using query-key-value matrices.

questions.question: How does self-attention differ from recurrent architectures?
questions.answer: Self-attention processes all tokens simultaneously enabling parallelization, while RNNs process tokens sequentially. Attention captures long-range dependencies more effectively.

questions.question: What is multi-head attention and why is it used?
questions.answer: Multi-head attention uses multiple attention heads in parallel to attend to information from different representation subspaces, allowing the model to capture various types of relationships.

questions.question: Explain the computational complexity of self-attention.
questions.answer: Self-attention has O(n²) complexity with sequence length due to computing dot products between all token pairs, which is faster than sequential RNN processing for long sequences.

questions.question: What are positional encodings and why are they necessary?
questions.answer: Positional encodings inject sequence order information since transformers process all positions simultaneously. They use sinusoidal functions or learned embeddings.

questions.question: How does the transformer architecture enable parallel training?
questions.answer: Transformers eliminate sequential dependencies by using attention instead of recurrence, allowing all positions to be processed simultaneously during both training and inference.

questions.question: What is the role of feed-forward networks in transformers?
questions.answer: Position-wise feed-forward networks apply the same transformation independently to each position, adding non-linearity and increasing model capacity after attention.

questions.question: Explain layer normalization in transformer models.
questions.answer: Layer normalization stabilizes training by normalizing inputs across features, applied before sublayers (pre-norm) or after (post-norm) to prevent gradient issues.

questions.question: What is the purpose of residual connections in transformers?
questions.answer: Residual connections help gradients flow through deep networks by adding input to output of each sublayer, enabling training of very deep transformer models.

questions.question: How do transformers handle long-range dependencies better than RNNs?
questions.answer: Transformers directly connect any two positions via attention with constant path length, while RNNs require O(n) sequential steps limiting long-range information flow.

questions.question: What is the BLEU score and how is it used in NLP?
questions.answer: BLEU is a metric evaluating machine translation quality by comparing n-gram overlap between generated and reference texts, with brevity penalty for short outputs.

questions.question: Explain the concept of transfer learning in NLP.
questions.answer: Transfer learning pre-trains models on large corpora then fine-tunes on specific tasks, allowing models to leverage general linguistic knowledge for downstream applications.

questions.question: What is the difference between encoder and decoder in transformers?
questions.answer: Encoders process input bidirectionally with self-attention; decoders generate output autoregressively using masked self-attention and cross-attention to encoder outputs.

questions.question: How does BERT's masked language modeling work?
questions.answer: BERT randomly masks input tokens and trains to predict them using bidirectional context, learning deep representations effective for various downstream tasks.

questions.question: What are the main advantages of transformer-based models?
questions.answer: Advantages include: parallel processing for faster training, better long-range dependency modeling, unified architecture for multiple tasks, and scalable to billions of parameters.
